title: Deep audit: 3 math/logic bugs fixed in retirement_mc, sector_rotation, economic_surprise

bugs_found: retirement_mc.py: geometric/arithmetic drift mismatch — daily_mu used target geometric return directly, causing realized geometric returns to be ~sigma^2/2 lower than intended (7.3% realized vs 9% target for all_equity). Over 30-year simulations, this understates portfolio growth by ~20%.

sector_rotation.py: composite score weights not renormalized when timeframes missing — sectors with <5 timeframes of data got artificially lower composite scores (e.g., 8.35 instead of 10.44 when 12m data absent), biasing sector rankings against newer ETFs.

economic_surprise.py: CPI (CPIAUCSL) level-based series causes systematic bearish bias — using raw price level for surprise means latest > trailing median ALWAYS for a trending series, creating permanent -0.11 bearish drag on composite score even with perfectly steady inflation.
observation.gap_identified: Monte Carlo simulation drift calibration, missing-data handling in composite scores, and trend-stationarity assumptions in surprise indices

bugs_fixed: retirement_mc.py: Added Jensen's inequality correction — arithmetic daily drift = (geometric_mu + sigma^2/2) / 252. Applied to both simulate_retirement() and compute_safe_withdrawal_rate(). Realized median return now converges to target geometric return.

sector_rotation.py: Extracted _compute_composite_score() function that renormalizes weights to sum to 1.0 when timeframes are missing. Sectors with shorter history now scored fairly against longer-history peers.

economic_surprise.py: Added 'use_change' flag for level-based series (CPIAUCSL). When set, computes surprise on month-over-month % change instead of raw level, removing the systematic trend bias. Display still shows original CPI level for readability.
implementation.feature_built: Extracted _compute_composite_score as testable helper function in sector_rotation.py

limitations: INDPRO (Industrial Production) is also a level-based index and could benefit from use_change treatment, though its growth rate is slow enough that bias is small
The retirement_mc sigma^2/2 correction assumes log-normal returns; the Student-t innovation used adds slight kurtosis that may need further calibration
Sector rotation composite score renormalization assumes missing timeframes are missing at random, not systematically (e.g., a new ETF always missing 12m)
assessment.self_critique: Could have audited more files (only 8 of 55 services read). The copula_tail.py student-t fitting has dead code (lines 261-267) that should be removed, but I focused on math bugs over cleanup.

next_steps: Audit copula_tail.py: remove dead code in student-t fitting (lines 261-267 compute bogus loglik via stats.gamma that is always overridden)
Consider adding use_change to INDPRO and MANEMP in economic_surprise.py if level-trend bias is observed
Validate retirement_mc safe withdrawal rates against published Bengen (1994) 4% rule benchmarks
Add integration test for sector_rotation with mocked yfinance data covering partial history
